topic: Someone discovers their friend has been lying about their job
raw_output:
[story 1]

The air in the coffee shop felt suddenly thin, the aroma of burnt sugar doing nothing to mask the tightness in my chest. Sarah was practically glowing, recounting her latest project with an air of effortless accomplishment. "Honestly, Mark, it's just so rewarding," she sighed, tapping her perfectly manicured nail on the table. I forced a smile, a brittle thing that didn't reach my eyes. Every word she spoke about her demanding, high-level marketing role felt like a direct strike against my own quiet existence. I watched the way her eyes sparkled when she talked about quarterly targets, a light I simply wasn't permitted to possess. When she mentioned the intricate client negotiations she handled, a cold knot tightened in my stomach. I wanted to ask, to probe, but the words caught, lodging behind my teeth. Instead, I focused intently on my lukewarm latte, tracing the condensation ring on the wooden table, feeling a sharp, unwelcome ache bloom in my ribs.

[story 2]

I found the email tucked away in the shared drive, a document detailing Sarah’s supposed responsibilities at that prestigious tech firm. The details were meticulous, the jargon dense and impressive. Reading it, a strange, hot flush crept up my neck. It wasn't anger, exactly; it was a profound sense of being sidelined. I reread the section about leading the international rollout, my jaw clenching so hard my molars ached. When Sarah later cheerfully described her "new venture" to the group, her voice carried a distinct, polished resonance that made me feel suddenly clumsy and muted. I shifted in my seat, pulling my sweater tighter around myself, suddenly hyper-aware of the slight tremor in my hands. I wanted to undermine her, to show everyone that the reality she presented was a carefully constructed facade, but all that escaped was a strained, overly bright laugh that sounded entirely hollow.

[story 3]

It happened over lunch. Sarah was recounting a recent promotion, her gestures broad and confident, her laughter ringing clearly in the bustling cafeteria. I kept my attention fixed on my sandwich, but the sound of her voice seemed to vibrate directly against my skin. When she described the salary increase and the scope of her new position, a deep, unpleasant churning started low in my abdomen. I noticed the way she subtly avoided looking at me when she spoke about the demanding travel schedule—a quick glance away that felt like a deliberate dismissal. My gaze flicked to her, lingering a fraction too long on the expensive watch she wore, and I felt a sudden, sharp need to deflate the entire scene. I cleared my throat, a dry, rasping sound, and tried to inject a note of casual inquiry into the conversation, but my voice came out thin and reedy, lacking any real conviction. The desire to possess that level of success, to have that kind of visibility, became a heavy weight pressing down on my shoulders.
